name: June Oven
short_description: A Wi-Fi-connected countertop convection oven that uses an internal camera and machine learning to recognize food and run an automatic cook program.
long_description: The June Oven is a smart countertop appliance with an internal HD camera, a built-in scale, and a core-temperature probe, paired with computer-vision models that identify commonly cooked foods and select a cook program automatically. It is a clear example of physical AI hiding inside an everyday domestic object: rather than a robot or a humanoid, intelligence is embedded in a familiar appliance form, sensing and acting on the physical task of cooking. As the system cooks more food it improves its recognition, and it streams live and time-lapse video to a companion app. Originally launched by the startup June and later acquired by Weber, it shows how ambient AI can fold into the kitchen as a quiet, task-specific assistant.

images.caption: The June Oven, a countertop smart convection oven with an internal camera and touchscreen.
images.alt_text: A black stainless-steel countertop oven with a glass door and a small touchscreen on the front, photographed against a white background.

sources.title: June Oven: 12-in-1 Countertop Convection Oven
sources.access_notes: Official product page describing the camera, Food ID recognition, and automatic cook programs.

sources.title: June (company)
sources.access_notes: Founding (2015), first-gen launch (December 2016), second gen (2018), and Weber acquisition (January 2021); computer-vision food identification details.

interactions.title: Drop in a tray and just press start
interactions.description: Slide in chicken thighs or a frozen pizza and the interior camera recognizes the food, counts the pieces, and pre-loads a cook program — you confirm and tap start instead of dialing in time and temperature.

interactions.title: Watch dinner cook from the couch
interactions.description: A ceiling-mounted heat-resistant camera streams live video of the food to the phone app, then stitches the session into a time-lapse you can replay once it's done — so you check on the roast without getting up.

interactions.title: Cook to a temperature, not a timer
interactions.description: Insert the food thermometer, pick a doneness, and the oven holds the program until the core hits target — auto-adjusting heat and pinging your phone the moment the meat is ready.

interactions.title: Preheat with your hands full
interactions.description: Mid-prep with messy hands, say "Alexa, tell June to preheat to 350" and the oven spins up — plus voice commands to set timers, check an ETA, or stop cooking without touching the panel.

interactions.title: Frozen pizza, recognized down to the crust
interactions.description: A reviewer dropped in a frozen pizza and June not only identified it but offered a thin-or-thick-crust choice, then ran the matching program — the vision model resolving the dish, not just the category.

interactions.title: It quietly replaces the big oven for small jobs
interactions.description: Owners report defaulting to the June for everyday batches — toast, roasted carrots, pizza rolls, salmon, cookies — using the camera to watch from another room and trusting it not to singe a pie crust or over-brown the nuts.

interactions.title: Snap a finished-dish card from the time-lapse
interactions.description: Because the camera already labels the food and films the whole bake, the app could auto-pick the best frame and stamp it with the dish name and cook time — a shareable card generated from sensing it already does, no extra photo needed.

interactions.title: Count-aware portion warnings
interactions.description: Since the on-device network counts the items on the tray, it could flag an overcrowded pan that won't crisp evenly and suggest splitting it across two racks before you commit to start.
